logs.summary:
Venezuela's Acting President Delcy Rodríguez publicly thanked Caricom countries for their aid as the death toll from the twin earthquakes remained at 4,829, with thousands homeless and families continuing to search rubble. The earthquake recovery effort has also overshadowed the country's push for democracy, according to analysis.

New reports detail ongoing recovery: the death toll remains at 4,490, while NASA radar data reveals significant ground shifts near La Guaira. Aid continues from Florida, Guyana, and Houston, bolstered by U.S. State Department support, and volunteers search tirelessly for missing victims. Lawmakers have urged lifting sanctions, and Venezuela’s inflation spike is compounding earthquake misery.

New reports as of July 14 show the earthquake death toll has risen to between 4,561 and 4,734. A Palm Beach County Fire Rescue team successfully rescued a man buried in rubble, and aid continues to flow from Florida and other regions to support recovery efforts.

logs.summary:
The death toll from the twin earthquakes has officially risen to 4,829, according to government figures. The European Union has pledged an additional €20 million in humanitarian aid, while US troops unloaded China's only known aid flight at the scene. Reports also highlight engineers assessing structural damage, a benefit concert featuring Marc Anthony and Ricardo Montaner, and ongoing grassroots fundraising efforts from Florida to Minnesota.

The death toll from the twin earthquakes was officially confirmed at 4,829 by National Assembly President Jorge Rodriguez. A fake image of a dog protecting a child went viral and was debunked by fact-checkers. Meanwhile, recovery efforts include a World Cup halftime tribute featuring the NY Philharmonic and Gustavo Dudamel, and an opinion piece highlights the prioritization of critical health services in the aftermath.

incidents.canonical_name: Earthquake 42 km NNW of Te Anau, New Zealand 2026-07
incidents.summary:
A magnitude 5.9 earthquake struck 42 km north-northwest of Te Anau, New Zealand, on July 16, 2026, initially reported as a 6.3 magnitude. The quake shook buildings across the South Island's tourist region and prompted a brief tsunami warning, which was later downgraded to a marine advisory. No injuries or damage were reported, and authorities lifted the tsunami alert shortly after.

A tsunami warning and immediate evacuation order were issued for parts of the South Island's west coast following the earthquake, but the alert has since been lifted. The mayor of the affected area reported property damage, and GeoNet recorded several lighter aftershocks after the main quake.
